---
title: "Cleaning Data That Changes Estimates"
---

## Purpose

Data cleaning is part of the analysis in creel work, not just housekeeping.

It can change the estimand, the totals, the rates, and the uncertainty.

That is true because cleaning decisions often change which records remain in scope and how the remaining records are interpreted. In creel analysis, a cleanup rule can be every bit as consequential as a modeling choice.

## Key ideas

- Species codes
- Trip duration
- Party size
- Zero catches
- Duplicate interviews
- Impossible values
- Missing or inconsistent trip boundaries

## Why cleaning is methodological

In creel work, a cleanup step can:

- change whether a record is included;
- change how a trip is counted;
- change the denominator used for a rate;
- change the species classification used in the report;
- change the uncertainty because the effective sample changes.

That means cleaning is part of the method, not just part of file preparation.

The analyst should think of cleaning as a sequence of methodological decisions:

- what counts as a valid record;
- what gets recoded versus removed;
- what can be repaired from the source data;
- and what should be left untouched because it would alter the original meaning.

If those decisions are made casually, the analysis can drift away from the field record without anyone noticing.

## Common cleanup tasks

Examples include:

- standardizing species codes;
- checking that trip start and end times are plausible;
- reconciling party size with angler counts;
- identifying duplicates;
- fixing impossible values;
- deciding how to handle zero-catch and zero-effort records;
- separating incomplete and completed trips correctly.

Each of these tasks can look mechanical, but each one carries inferential consequences. For example, standardizing species codes sounds clerical until a code merge changes the species-level total in the final report.

## What to document

Cleanup should leave a trail. The analyst should be able to answer:

- which records were changed;
- which rule was applied;
- whether the raw record was preserved;
- and whether the same rule will be used in the next reporting cycle.

That log is part of the analysis record. It protects the estimate from becoming an undocumented sequence of edits.

## A conservative rule

When a cleanup choice changes the estimate, it should be documented the same way a modeling choice would be documented.

That means the analyst should be able to answer:

- what was changed;
- why it was changed;
- what the effect on the estimate was;
- and whether the same choice should be applied in future reports.

When the impact is large, the safest workflow is usually to keep both the raw and cleaned versions and make the transformation explicit rather than irreversible.

## `tidycreel` support

Implemented:
- Later chapters show which validation and cleaning helpers are available in the package workflow.

Partially supported:
- The conceptual rule set is introduced here; implementation details come later.

Manual or conceptual:
- Deciding how cleaning affects the estimand.
- Writing a cleanup log.
- Preserving the raw record while creating a cleaned analysis record.

The package can assist with validation and downstream analysis, but it cannot decide which records deserve to be altered. That judgment belongs to the analyst.

## Example focus

How a seemingly minor cleanup changes an annual harvest estimate by altering the denominator and the number of included interviews.

That example matters because it shows why a cleanup rule should be tested like any other methodological choice. If the estimate changes materially, the report should say so.

## Takeaway

Cleaning is part of inference. If a cleanup rule changes the estimate, it deserves to be documented, reviewed, and justified.

In creel work, the boundary between data preparation and analysis is thinner than it looks.
